metadata.name: Travertine
metadata.title: Travertine Settings
metadata.description: When laser cleaning Travertine, watch out for its high porosity right from the start—I've seen it soak up heat unevenly, which can lead to micro-cracks if you're not careful. This natural stone, like a spongy limestone, differs from denser rocks because it traps contaminants deep inside, so you need to go gentle to avoid pulling the surface apart. Tends to heat up slowly due to poor conductivity, meaning prolonged exposure risks surface spalling. Start with lower power settings and slower scan speeds to let the laser gently loosen dirt without stressing the pores. This works best when you overlap passes lightly, pulling grime out layer by layer. I've found multiple light treatments clear buildup effectively while preserving the stone's texture for heritage sites. Just avoid high energy bursts—they'll scorch the calcite base and ruin that classic banded look. Adjust based on soiling thickness, and always test a small area first to keep things safe.

author.name: Yi-Chun Lin

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Travertine surface cleaning without thermal damage

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength optimized for Travertine absorption and minimal substrate damage

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for balanced cleaning efficiency and resolution

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning coverage and process speed

energyDensity.description: Fluence level sufficient for contaminant removal below Travertine damage threshold

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for effective contaminant removal with controlled thermal penetration

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning without overlapping damage

passCount.description: Number of cleaning passes for complete contaminant removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for complete coverage without excessive energy deposition

power.description: Typical range for pulsed Nd:YAG lasers (1064 nm) on porous stones like travertine to remove contaminants without thermal damage; starting at 50 W for light soiling, max 200 W risks microcracking (source: IPG Photonics laser cleaning guidelines and studies on limestone ablation, e.g., J. Cult. Herit. 2018).

scan_speed.description: Effective speeds balance fluence and coverage; 1000 mm/s optimal for uniform cleaning on travertine, slower (500 mm/s) for heavy contamination, faster (up to 3000 mm/s) for light dust (source: Industry standards from Laser Cleaning Europe and equipment manuals like those from CleanLaser).

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective cleaning with minimal heat-affected zone on heat-sensitive travertine; 20 ns balances ablation efficiency and reduced thermal diffusion (picosecond/fs for ultra-precision but overkill; source: Laser physics research in Opt. Lasers Eng. 2020 on stone heritage cleaning).

repetition_rate.description: Typical for industrial cleaning to achieve pulse overlap without excessive heat buildup in low-conductivity travertine; 30 kHz recommended for faster processing vs. heat risks at higher rates (source: Academic literature, e.g., Appl. Surf. Sci. 2019 on pulsed laser parameters for carbonates).

energy_density.description: Fluence range for contaminant removal (e.g., soot, oxides) on travertine; damage threshold ~2-3 J/cm² for CaCO3-based stone, so 1.0 J/cm² safe starting point to avoid spalling (source: Ablation threshold studies in J. Appl. Phys. 2017 and heritage conservation papers).

spot_size.description: Beam diameter for effective energy distribution; 200 μm recommended for travertine to cover pores without pinpoint damage, smaller for fine details, larger for broad areas (source: Equipment specs from manufacturers like Raycus and Coherent laser systems).

pass_count.description: Multi-pass approach ensures complete removal without exceeding fluence limits on delicate travertine; 2 passes typical for industrial surface prep (single pass for very light contamination; source: Industry case studies in stone restoration, e.g., Restaurators 2021).

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform coverage and prevents stripe patterns on uneven travertine surfaces; 70% optimal for balance between efficiency and overlap-induced heating (source: Process optimization in laser scanning studies, e.g., Opt. Express 2016).
